content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Lukas on UnsplashIf you’re starting your journey into Linux, one of the most confusing things you’ll see is a bunch of directories sitting under the root (/) directory.
/
├── bin
├── boot
├── dev
├── etc
├── home
├── lib
├── media
├── mnt
├── opt
├── proc
├── root
├── run
├── sbin
├── srv
├── sys
├── tmp
├── usr
└── varAt first glance, none of these names tell you much.
Why is there a /usr directory if you're already inside Linux? What's the difference between /bin and /sbin? Why does /proc contain files even though nothing is actually stored there?
I remember memorizing these directories when I first started learning Linux, but memorization didn’t help much. It wasn’t until I started working with Docker containers, Kubernetes clusters, servers, and production deployments that these directories finally made sense.
In this article, we’ll walk through the Linux directories you’ll encounter most often, explain what each one is for, and look at real-world examples of when you’ll actually use them.

Before We Start: Everything Begins at /
Unlike Windows, Linux doesn’t have multiple drive letters like C:\ or D:\.
Everything starts from a single directory called the root directory.
/Every file, every application, every configuration, every mounted disk, and every user directory eventually lives somewhere beneath this single location.
Think of it like the trunk of a tree. Every other directory is simply another branch growing from it.

1. /home --- User Home Directories
If someone creates a normal user account, this is where their personal files live.
/home
├── alice
├── bob
└── monikInside your own home directory you’ll usually find things like:
Documents/
Downloads/
Pictures/
Desktop/
Projects/You’ll also see hidden files beginning with a dot.
.bashrc
.gitconfig
.sshThese store your personal configuration.
Example:
cd /home/monik
ls -la2. /root --- The Administrator's Home
The /root directory is the home folder of the root (administrator) user.
/root3. /etc --- System Configuration
Most system-wide configuration files live here.
Examples:

• /etc/passwd

• /etc/hosts

• /etc/fstab

• /etc/ssh/

• /etc/nginx/

• /etc/systemd/
4. /var --- Data That Changes Frequently
Stores logs, caches, databases, and runtime application data.
Examples:

• /var/log

• /var/cache

• /var/lib

• /var/tmp
Docker data commonly lives in /var/lib/docker.
5. /tmp --- Temporary Files
Temporary files created by applications. Many distributions clean this directory automatically after reboot.
6. /usr --- User Programs and Libraries
Contains most installed applications and libraries.
Common directories:

• /usr/bin

• /usr/lib

• /usr/share

• /usr/local
7. /bin --- Essential User Commands
Contains essential executables such as:

• ls

• cp

• mv

• cat

• mkdir

• rm
8. /sbin --- System Administration Commands
Contains administrative utilities like:

• mount

• shutdown

• reboot

• fsck
9. /dev --- Devices as Files
Linux represents devices as files.
Examples:

• /dev/sda

• /dev/null

• /dev/random

• /dev/tty
10. /proc --- Information About Running Processes
A virtual filesystem exposing kernel and process information.
Useful commands:
cat /proc/cpuinfo
cat /proc/meminfo
cat /proc/version11. /sys --- Hardware Information
A virtual filesystem exposing hardware and kernel interfaces.
12. /opt --- Optional Software
Third-party applications are commonly installed here.
13. /boot --- Boot Loader Files
Contains the Linux kernel, initramfs, and bootloader files.
14. /mnt and /media --- Mounted Storage

• /media is typically used for removable devices.

• /mnt is commonly used for manual or temporary mounts.
15. /run --- Runtime Information
Stores temporary runtime data such as PID files and sockets.

Final Thoughts
When I first started using Linux, I tried to memorize directory names. That worked just well enough to pass a quiz, but it didn’t help me troubleshoot real systems.
Once you understand the purpose of these directories, the Linux filesystem stops feeling like a random collection of folders and starts to feel like a well-organized map. Whether you’re debugging a server, managing Docker containers, or working with Kubernetes, this knowledge pays off every day.
